- **C — Recategorize → `principle`** (stated negatively). Keeps them standalone but stops calling them patterns.

## Recommendation

A (keep + cross-link). Non-binding until you decide.

## See also

_`docs/research/library-template-alignment.md` §7 D2; ADR-0008, ADR-0009. Related: `approval-gated-trunk`, `claims-in-the-shared-store`, `durable-workflow-per-node`._

title: lifecycle status
description: A unit's status, drawn from six states — proposed, building, healthy, unhealthy, mapped, retired — that track it from authoring to proven to pruned.
body:
**In one line.** A unit's status, drawn from six states — proposed, building, healthy, unhealthy, mapped, retired — that track it from authoring through proven to pruned.

## What it is

Status lives on every tier (story / capability / contract) and is drawn from six states:

- **proposed** — authored but not yet selected for implementation; the initial state.

- **building** — selected and under active implementation (v1: `under_construction`); written at pickup as the first commit, before any code edits.

- **healthy** — proven: the unit reached `healthy` through its tier's proof mode at HEAD — a story by a UAT pass over fresh green capabilities, a capability by integration tests over fresh green contracts, a contract by its isolated unit test (or, where neither honest test exists, operator-attested) (ADR-0010).

- **unhealthy** — a once-healthy capability that has drifted (a contract test now fails, owned files changed, or the proof no longer matches HEAD); **computed** from evidence, never written to disk.

- **mapped** — brownfield: the capability is *observationally* verified by an existing target-repo test suite, without storytree driving a red→green flow; a distinct, weaker state than `healthy` — observational green never short-circuits to proven; v2 **supports** brownfield, the exact mapping mechanism under the owned loop / DBOS is still to design (see `open-questions.md` §2).

- **retired** — terminal off-tree state: pruned from the active tree; may carry `retired_reason` (prose) and `superseded_by` (an edge to its replacement).

## What it is not

`healthy` is not `mapped` (observational green never short-circuits to proven) and not `unhealthy` (a once-healthy unit that has since drifted). `mapped` and `unhealthy` are computed from evidence, never written to disk.

## See also

_`docs/glossary.md`; ADR-0010 (organism model); `open-questions.md` §2 (mapping mechanism open). Related: `proof-mode`, `node-rollup`, `proof-hash`, `evidence`, `operator-attested`, `prove-it-gate`._

title: unit fields
description: The four core fields a unit carries — outcome, guidance, title, id — covering its value statement, rebuild context, human label, and unique identifier.
body:
**In one line.** The four core fields a unit carries — outcome, guidance, title, id — covering its value statement, rebuild context, human label, and unique identifier.

## What it is

The four core fields a unit carries:

- **outcome** — a capability's plain-English, single-sentence value statement, with no conjunctions (if it needs them, split the unit instead).

- **guidance** — non-obvious technical context needed to rebuild a unit; only what an agent could not derive from outcome + proof.

- **title** — a short human label for a unit; not load-bearing for proof.

- **id** — a unit's unique identifier. v2 must allocate these **conflict-free across concurrent sessions** — a stated goal. An earlier DBOS spike showed durable, collision-free workflow IDs as one candidate mechanism, but DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so that mechanism is reference, not the adopted id-allocation path.

## See also

_From `docs/glossary.md`._

title: Cloud SQL access · Node connector · keyless IAM
description: How storytree reaches its Postgres runtime store: the Cloud SQL Node connector with IAM database auth via ambient ADC. Agent sessions need no per-session OAuth and no key files.
body:
**The choice.** Reach the Cloud SQL Postgres via the Node connector with keyless IAM database authentication — agent sessions authenticate from ambient Application Default Credentials, never a per-session OAuth or a key file.

## What it is

The `@google-cloud/cloud-sql-connector` Node connector (paired with `pg`, in `packages/store`) mints short-lived IAM tokens as the database password over a TLS socket — no password is stored. Identity comes from the operator’s Application Default Credentials already established on the dev host (plus a dedicated service account for headless/impersonated runs). The IAM database user is the operator email (`STORYTREE_DB_USER`), the database is `storytree`, and the instance connection name is `storytree-498613:australia-southeast1:storytree-pg`.

## Why this

No secrets in the repo and no per-session interactive login, so agents can run migrations and queries headlessly; it matches the stop/start cost posture (ADR-0015 §5) and keeps the browser/credential-free boundary (the studio never holds DB creds). A session should ASSUME keyless credentials are present and verify with `gcloud auth application-default print-access-token`, not assume it is unauthenticated.

## Constraints

The instance is STOPPED by default and bills only while up — burst with `pnpm db:up` → work → `pnpm db:down`. Auto-stop is **idle-aware** (ADR-0015 §5, `infra/idle-stop.tf`): a Cloud Function stops the instance only after ~60 min with zero DB connections, so it never stops a live session (a connection held by a session / the Auth Proxy resets the idle timer); a once-a-day blunt cron is the cost floor behind it. So a long idle gap will stop the DB — re-`db:up` if a query fails to connect. IAM users are bare roles, so a ONE-TIME privilege bootstrap (`infra/bootstrap-grants.sql`, applied as the throwaway-password `postgres` user via `packages/store/scripts/apply-grants.mjs`) granted the IAM user `CREATE` on the database; this is idempotent and already applied. The connector deps live in `packages/store`; DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection.

## See also

_Synthesised from ADR-0021 (doc:decisions/0021-keyless-agent-session-auth-and-db-bootstrap.md) and ADR-0015 (doc:decisions/0015-gcp-hosting-cloud-sql-event-store.md). Validated 2026-06-08: 73 units migrated keyless, then the instance stopped._

title: Resolve, supersede, or retire draft ADR-0014?
description: ADR-0014 (notice-board feedback graduates into durable guidance) is still `draft`, yet the comments->events / studio<->store migration it anticipated is now being built — its status and surviving items need a call.
body:
**Why this matters.** A decision is still stamped “draft” while the work it describes is already being built — like a policy stuck in “pending sign-off” after the team has moved on to a newer version of it. Until we file it, people keep citing an unsettled decision as if it were final.

## The question

ADR-0014 (notice-board feedback -> durable guidance) is still `draft` with unresolved items, while its comments->events and studio<->store migration is now being built — should it be resolved (accepted/superseded), or retired, and which of its open items survive?

## Context

ADR-0014 framed how forum comments graduate into durable Library guidance. The migration it depends on is landing now: comments are moving into the event store (`events.comment`, with an `events.comment_event` table recording every change to a comment) and the studio is being wired to the shared Postgres store. A `draft` ADR the implementation has already overtaken is a drift risk — the build can diverge from a decision that was never settled, and downstream units cite it as if accepted. Its open items overlap newer ADRs (0017, which owns the knowledge/Library tier; 0018, the open-question → ADR resolution flow), so some may already be answered.

## Diagram

```
  ADR-0014 (draft) ── describes ──► comments → events.comment migration
        │                            + studio ↔ Postgres wiring  (building NOW)
        │
        └─ open items overlap ──► ADR-0017  (owns the knowledge/Library tier)
                                  ADR-0018  (open-question → ADR flow)

  ⇒ lean: supersede 0014 BY 0017/0018, carry any still-live item forward
```
The newer ADRs already own what 0014 was reaching for; superseding keeps the lineage while the build stops citing a draft.

## Options

- **A — Accept as-is**: ratify ADR-0014's current text and clear its open items. Cheapest paper-trail, but risks blessing claims the migration has already changed.

- **B — Supersede**: mark it superseded by ADR-0017/0018 (which own the knowledge tier and the open-question->ADR graduation), carrying forward only the still-live items as new open-questions. Cleanest lineage; most editing.

- **C — Retire**: withdraw ADR-0014 entirely as overtaken by events, and re-raise any genuinely-open item fresh. Least history to maintain, but loses the original rationale.

## Recommendation

Non-binding: lean B (supersede by ADR-0017/0018). The comments->events + studio<->store work is exactly what 0017/0018 govern, so 0014's durable-guidance intent is now carried there; supersede preserves lineage while the surviving items become first-class open-questions decided in the studio. The owner decides.

## See also

_ADR-0014 (doc:decisions/0014-notice-board-feedback-graduates-into-durable-guidance.md); supersession candidates ADR-0017 (knowledge tier) and ADR-0018 (open-question resolution). Related: `oq-library-doc-shape`._

title: How should the studio reconcile structured docs vs rendered edits?
description: The DB stores structured Knowledge docs, but the studio writes rendered GuidanceAssets, so editing a unit in the studio converts it to rendered form (one-way). Decide how structure survives editing.
body:
**Why this matters.** Edit a Library entry in the studio today and it silently loses its structured fields — like an employee opening a fill-in-the-blanks form and the system saving it back as a freeform note with the field labels stripped out. The next rebuild from source then fights that edit, and one wins by accident.

## The question

The store holds structured Knowledge docs (per-kind fields), but the studio writes rendered GuidanceAssets — so editing a unit in the studio collapses it to a rendered markdown body (one-way). How should the structured authoring source survive editing?

## Context

The studio’s save path (the `pg` write) stores each entry as a single rendered-markdown `body` and validates that string; the per-field structure (oneLine / whatItIs / options / …) is rebuilt on read by `renderBody()` — the core function that turns structured fields into the markdown you see — and CANNOT be recovered from the rendered body without parsing it back. `knowledge.json` stays the structured authoring source, but once an entry is edited in the studio its stored form is rendered-only, diverging from that source — which `build-corpus.mjs` (the generator that rebuilds the studio’s data from `knowledge.json`) would then overwrite or conflict with. ADR-0013 (“structured corpus, markdown as a view”) wants structure-as-source; ADR-0017 (the knowledge/Library tier) makes the templates derive from the schema. Editing in rendered form fights both.

## Diagram

```
  knowledge.json ──build-corpus.mjs──► database row ──renderBody()──► fields shown
  (structured       (generator)          (rendered body,   (rebuilds the   in the studio
   source)                                structure dropped) field view)
       ▲                                       ▲
       │                                       │ studio EDIT saves a rendered body
       │  next rebuild CONFLICTS with ─────────┘ (one-way: the structured fields are gone)
       │  whatever the studio saved
```
`build-corpus.mjs` = the script that turns `knowledge.json` into what the studio reads; `renderBody()` = the function that rebuilds the field view on read.

## Options

- **A — Accept rendered-on-edit + re-seed**: let studio edits store rendered bodies, and keep `knowledge.json` as the structured source that re-seeds structure on demand. Simplest to build (matches the current pg write); but edited units lose structured fields until re-seeded, and the two sources can drift.

- **B — Parse markdown back to structured on write**: the studio write reparses the rendered body into per-kind fields (inverse of `renderBody`) and stores a structured doc. Keeps one structured source; but the parser is fragile and must round-trip every kind exactly.

- **C — Edit structured fields directly**: the studio edits the per-kind fields (not a freeform body), so writes are always structured. Truest to ADR-0013/0017; largest client change (the wire shape the React client sees would have to carry fields, not just `body`).

## Recommendation

Non-binding: ship A now (it matches the additive, reversible pg write being built and keeps the client wire shape unchanged), and track B/C as the proper fix once the studio<->store loop is proven. A keeps `knowledge.json` authoritative for structure while unblocking shared live state. The owner decides.

## See also

_ADR-0017 (doc:decisions/0017-cross-cutting-knowledge-tier.md) and ADR-0013 (doc:decisions/0013-structured-corpus-markdown-as-view.md). Related: `oq-corpus-source-format`, `oq-studio-store-default`._

title: Should STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE default to json or pg?
description: The studio backend is env-toggled between the offline `json` store and the live Cloud SQL `pg` store. Decide the default the toggle ships with.
body:
**Why this matters.** When you start the studio, should it default to your own private copy of the data — works offline and costs nothing, but every session drifts apart — or the shared company database, where everyone sees the same thing but it must be switched on first and bills while running? `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` is that switch, and we need to pick its default.

## The question

Should `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` default to `json` (offline-safe — the DB stays stopped, no cost, per-worktree state) or `pg` (shared live state across sessions — requires `pnpm db:up` and bills while up)?

## Context

The studio backend is being made switchable so the same `/api/*` contract serves either the offline `data/*.json` files or the live Postgres store. `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` is that switch, and its default decides what a plain `pnpm dev` does. ADR-0015 keeps the Cloud SQL database STOPPED by default and bills only while it is up (you burst it on with `pnpm db:up`, work, then `pnpm db:down`); keyless IAM auth (ADR-0021) means there are no credentials to configure, but `pg` still needs the instance running. `json` is offline-safe but gives each worktree its own divergent copy — the very thing this migration aims to end.

## Diagram

```
  pnpm dev (fresh start)
        │
        ├── default json ──► offline, $0, the DB stays off
        │                    BUT each session keeps its own copy → they drift apart
        │
        └── default pg ────► shared live state (the migration's goal)
                             BUT needs `pnpm db:up` first, and bills while up
```
The default trades “just works offline, but everyone diverges” against “shared by default, but costs and needs the DB up.”

## Options

- **A — Default `json`**: offline-safe, zero cost, no DB prerequisite; a fresh clone just works. But shared live state (the migration's goal) is opt-in, so most sessions keep diverging.

- **B — Default `pg`**: sessions share live state by default — the dogfooding win. But `pnpm dev` now fails unless the instance is up (cost + a `db:up` step), breaking offline work.

## Recommendation

Non-binding: default `json` while the pg path is new and human-verified-after-the-fact — it preserves offline dev and the stop-by-default cost posture, and `pg` stays a deliberate opt-in for sessions that want shared state. Revisit once the studio<->store loop is proven and `db:up` is routine. The owner decides.

## See also

_ADR-0015 (doc:decisions/0015-gcp-hosting-cloud-sql-event-store.md) stop/start cost posture; ADR-0021 (keyless IAM). Related: `oq-library-doc-shape`, `stack-cloud-sql-keyless-iam`._

title: Reconcile the YAML-as-source claim with the JSON/markdown reality?
description: ADR-0013 says YAML is the source of truth, but the Library's structured source on disk is JSON (knowledge.json) and stories/ are frontmatter-.md. Reconcile the formats or document the split as intended.
body:
**Why this matters.** Our filing policy (ADR-0013) says every record is kept in YAML, but the Library actually files in JSON and the stories in markdown — an employee handbook naming a filing system nobody uses. Anyone who trusts the handbook hunts for YAML that isn’t there, and any tool built on that assumption breaks.

## The question

ADR-0013 says 'YAML is the source of truth', but the Library's structured source on disk is JSON (`apps/studio/data/knowledge.json`) and the `stories/` corpus is frontmatter-`.md` — should the formats be reconciled (commit to one) or the split documented as intended?

## Context

ADR-0013 (“structured corpus, markdown as a view”) asserts YAML as the authoring source of truth. In practice the Library authors in JSON (`knowledge.json`, consumed by the `build-corpus.mjs` generator) and the story tier authors in frontmatter-markdown (`stories/`). No YAML is the source of either. The mismatch is a documentation/decision drift: a reader of ADR-0013 expects YAML and finds none, and tooling that assumed YAML would be wrong. Either the ADR is stale, or the per-tier format split is deliberate and just undocumented.

## Diagram

```
  ADR-0013 says:  "YAML is the source of truth"  ──on disk──►  (no YAML anywhere)

  Library tier  ──source──►  knowledge.json          ──build-corpus.mjs──►  rendered
                             (JSON)                                          markdown view
  Story tier    ──source──►  stories/*.md             ─────────────────────►  rendered
                             (frontmatter-markdown)                            as-is
```
Reality is a per-tier split (JSON + markdown), not YAML — the cheap fix is to make the ADR say so.

## Options

- **A — Commit to one format**: pick a single structured source format (e.g. JSON everywhere, or YAML everywhere) and migrate, then ADR-0013 is literally true. Cleanest mental model; real migration cost and churn in build tooling.

- **B — Document the split as intended**: amend ADR-0013 to state that the structured source is JSON for the Library and frontmatter-`.md` for stories (markdown still the rendered view), and that 'YAML' was illustrative. No migration; the ADR matches reality. Accepts heterogeneity.

- **C — Defer**: leave it, accept the drift for now. Cheapest, but the stale ADR keeps misleading readers.

## Recommendation

Non-binding: B (document the split). The JSON/markdown split already works and feeds the generators; the cheap, honest fix is to update ADR-0013 to describe the per-tier formats rather than force a migration whose only benefit is making one sentence literally true. The owner decides.

## See also

_ADR-0013 (doc:decisions/0013-structured-corpus-markdown-as-view.md) the YAML-as-source claim; ADR-0017 (knowledge tier, structured source). Related: `oq-library-doc-shape`._
